memos.snapshot: Observability for ML feature pipelines. Claims $1.2M ARR and 22% MoM growth, but the deck figure is contradicted by the Stripe export and one reference call.

sections.title: Company snapshot
sections.content: Observability for ML feature pipelines — drift detection, lineage, and replay for teams running training jobs in production. Seed-stage, Berlin, four full-time engineers.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Team of 4 FTE, Berlin, incorporated March 2025.
Incorporation date and registered officers corroborate the deck.

sections.title: Problem & product
sections.content: Feature pipelines fail silently — a schema drift upstream degrades a model for weeks before anyone notices. NovaMetrics instruments the pipeline itself rather than the model output, which is what lets them catch drift at the source.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Architecture diagram: agents instrument Airflow and Dagster operators directly.
Two design partners confirmed the silent-failure problem independently.

sections.title: Traction
sections.content: CONTRADICTION: the deck claims $1.2M ARR as of March 2026 with 22% MoM growth. The Stripe export supplied in diligence totals $412K ARR for the same period, and reference call 02 described revenue as "just under half a million". The gap is unexplained and material.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Claims $1.2M ARR as of March 2026; chart shows 22% MoM growth.
Gross recurring revenue totals $412,000 annualised for the same period.
Founder described revenue as "just under half a million".

memos.snapshot: Deterministic replay for distributed training jobs. Second-time founder, 2x prior exit verified via filings; 312 commits in the trailing 90 days.

sections.title: Company snapshot
sections.content: Deterministic replay and checkpointing for multi-node training runs. Solo technical founder, pre-seed, currently three design partners in enterprise ML platform teams.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Solo founder, pre-seed, three named design partners.

sections.title: Problem & product
sections.content: A failed training run at hour 40 currently means restarting from the last checkpoint and hoping the failure does not recur. Rivera Labs makes runs bit-reproducible so a failure can be replayed and diagnosed rather than merely retried.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Core replay engine rewritten solo by the founder over 11 days.

sections.title: Traction
sections.content: Three design partners, no revenue yet by the founder's own statement. Deliberately pre-revenue — the team is optimising for depth of integration before pricing.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Founder stated no revenue and explained the reasoning without prompting.

sections.title: Hypotheses
sections.content: If reproducibility becomes a compliance requirement for regulated ML deployments, this moves from a developer convenience to a procurement checkbox. The bet is on that regulatory shift arriving within 24 months.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Record-keeping obligations for high-risk systems may imply run reproducibility.

sections.title: SWOT
sections.content: Strength: rare systems depth, 2x prior exit verified via filings. Weakness: solo founder, no commercial hire. Opportunity: compliance-driven demand. Threat: the major training frameworks could absorb this natively.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Two prior exits confirmed against the founder's stated history.

memos.snapshot: Attribution modelling for mid-market e-commerce. Sourced via a 2-hop network path; founder axis is weak and traction claims remain unconfirmed.

sections.title: Company snapshot
sections.content: Multi-touch attribution for e-commerce brands in the $5-50M revenue band. Two co-founders, both commercial rather than technical.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Two named co-founders, both with agency backgrounds.

sections.title: Problem & product
sections.content: Post-cookie attribution is genuinely broken for mid-market brands who cannot afford a data team. The product is a hosted model with pre-built connectors. The wedge is real; the differentiation against incumbents is not yet clear from available evidence.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Twelve advertised connectors, no published methodology.

memos.snapshot: Compiler toolchain for sparse tensor kernels. Discovered on GitHub, 1.2k stars in nine days; pricing thesis still rests on a single pilot.

sections.title: Company snapshot
sections.content: Compiler toolchain that generates hardware-specific kernels for sparse tensor operations. Discovered through GitHub sourcing rather than inbound.

evidence.evidence_snippet: 1,240 stars in the nine days following first public release.

sections.title: Problem & product
sections.content: Sparse kernels are hand-tuned per architecture, so a model that is fast on one accelerator is slow on the next. LatticeForge compiles from a single description to per-target kernels.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Benchmarks show 3.1x over hand-tuned baselines on two of three targets.

sections.title: Traction
sections.content: One paid pilot with a semiconductor vendor; strong open-source adoption that has not yet converted to revenue. Adoption is real but monetisation is unproven.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Single paid pilot confirmed; no second contract in progress.

sections.title: SWOT
sections.content: Strength: genuine technical moat and organic developer pull. Weakness: no commercial motion. Opportunity: accelerator vendors need this. Threat: a vendor builds it in-house and open-sources it.

memos.snapshot: Edge orchestration for low-latency inference. Deck parsed but most sections are still gaps — the founder withheld revenue and headcount.

sections.title: Company snapshot
sections.content: Orchestration layer that places inference workloads across edge points of presence. Deck received 40 minutes ago; diligence has not started.

evidence.evidence_snippet: Positioning statement and target latency envelope.
